title: Bound States in Second-order Topological Graphitic Structures
authors: Haiyue Huang, Prineha Narang, Ioannis Petrides
abstract: Quadrupole insulators are a class of second-order topological insulators (SOTIs) that host zero-dimensional corner states within a two-dimensional bulk. Despite their unique properties, their realization in electronic systems on realistic material platforms remains rare. In this work, we present a general design principle to obtain quadrupole insulators based on two-dimensional graphitic structures. By engineering the positions and connections of zigzag edges, we identify four topological classes of graphitic structures. We show that topologically protected massless corner state emerge at the intersection of domains belonging to different topological classes. Crucially, by tuning the smoothness of the domain wall, we further demonstrate the appearance of additional massive localized states with non-zero angular momentum. Our results provide a practical framework for realizing experimentally accessible SOTIs and uncover the coexistence of both massless and massive bound states in two dimensions.
summary: This paper provides a practical framework for creating quadrupole insulators by engineering the edges of 2D graphitic structures. It reveals that the smoothness of domain walls can be used to control the presence of both massless and massive localized corner states. This work offers a way to realize and manipulate second-order topological phases in experimentally accessible carbon-based platforms.
main_problem: The difficulty in realizing and controlling second-order topological insulators (SOTIs), specifically quadrupole insulators, in realistic electronic material platforms and understanding the role of domain wall smoothness.
main_result: The authors present a design principle for quadrupole insulators using 2D graphitic structures and demonstrate that tuning domain wall smoothness allows for the coexistence of massless and massive corner states.
method: The study employs Density Functional Theory (DFT), tight-binding modeling, Wilson loop/Wannier center analysis, and analytical continuum theory to investigate topological properties.
model_or_system: The system consists of 2D graphitic structures engineered via zigzag edges, modeled by an effective tight-binding Hamiltonian based on SSH chains and a p-orbital-based Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Quadrupole topological charge, Wannier-sector polarization, corner state energy (massless vs. massive), and angular momentum of localized states.
important_parameters: Hopping strengths (intra-cell and inter-cell), domain wall height and spread, and mirror symmetry breaking perturbations.
main_assumptions: The effective model assumes nearest-neighbor hopping between p_z orbitals and focuses on the low-energy physics around the Dirac cone.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the four topological classes of graphitic unit cells; subsequent figures illustrate band structures, Wannier bands, and the spatial distribution of corner state wavefunctions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the topological classes, establishes an effective Hamiltonian, validates the model using DFT, explores domain wall physics, and analyzes the effect of symmetry breaking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The relative strength of intra-cell (Jµ1) and inter-cell (Jµ2) hopping along the x and y directions give rise to four topological classes of graphitic structures. (a–d) are repre- sentative unit cells made of conjugated carbon atoms belong- ing to each class, with corresponding simplified tight-binding models illustrated in the insets. Orange shades mark zigzag edges and blue lines are boundaries of unit cells. Solid and dashed lines indicate positive (negative) sign of the hopping parameter. Thick (thin) lines indicate a larger (smaller) hop- ping strength. Carbon atoms on the edge are saturated with hydrogen atoms, which are not displayed.

FIG. 2. Electronic band structures calculated with DFT method of (a) type A and (b) type B graphitic unit cells show middle bands separated from low-/high-lying bands. By cal- culating Wilson loop along the x and y directions on the two occupied middle bands (blue shaded), two Wannier bands are obtained. (c, d) and (e, f) are Wannier bands for the unit cell A1 and B1, respectively. Note there is a small gap in (d) at k = π/a. Energy values are given relative to the Fermi level.

FIG. 3. (a) Periodic x-ribbon structure made of Ny = 40 repeating unit cells with open boundary condition along y for unit cell A1. (b) By calculating Wilson loop, their Wannier centers for the 2Ny bands below Fermi level are obtained, showing pairs of edge states (red arrows). Due to the existence of Mx, edge states are degenerate with quantized vx = 0 or 0.5. (c) Tangential polarization of the ribbon plot against distance along y, where the integrated polarization is 0.5|e|. Same plots for other unit cells can be found in Supplemental Material. (d) Supercell structure consisting of four distinct regions, each containing 16 × 16 graphitic unit cells. Regions corresponding to different...

FIG. 4. (a) The analytical results are verified by numerically solving artificial tight-binding models constructed from unit cells with four basis sites. Domain walls with intermediate values of µµ are inserted between adjacent regions along both x and y directions. (b) The spectrum of the artificial Hamil- tonian exhibits four in-gap corner states (red) and sixteen additional localized states (blue, eight shown), in agreement with the analytical predictions. (c) Charge distribution of the sixteen massive states. (d) For graphitic structures, only x-directional domain walls can be adjusted, owing to struc- tural constraints.
